steps.title: Patterns in pairs
steps.body: When two variables are measured together, scatterplots reveal direction and strength of association. You'll interpret correlation, fit regression lines, make predictions, and recognize when correlation does not imply causation.

definitions.definition: A graph of paired (x, y) points used to see the direction and strength of a relationship.
A number from −1 to +1 measuring the direction and strength of a *linear* relationship.
The best-fit line ŷ = a + bx used to predict y from x.
The predicted change in y for each one-unit increase in x.
Predicting inside the observed range (safe) vs outside it (unreliable).
The fraction of the variation in y explained by the model — the square of r.
A strong r shows association, not that one variable causes the other; watch for lurking variables.

steps.title: Match scatterplots to r
steps.prompt: Assign each correlation coefficient to the plot it matches.
hints: Sign of r matches direction (uphill = positive, downhill = negative). Magnitude matches how tightly points hug a line.
The shapeless blob is near 0. The tightest band gets the most extreme |r| — compare A's tightness to D's.

feedback.wrong: Read sign from slope direction and strength from scatter.

solution.body: **A = −0.95, B = −0.4, C = 0.0, D = 0.8.** Tight downhill → −0.95; loose downhill → −0.4; no trend → 0; tight uphill → 0.8.
solution.takeaway: Read sign from direction and strength from scatter; r near ±1 means tight linear packing, r near 0 means no linear trend.

steps.title: Predict the sign of r
steps.context: For each pair, would you expect r to be positive, negative, or near zero?

options.label: Car weight & fuel economy (mpg)
Hours studied & exam score
Shoe size & exam score

hints: Ask: as one variable rises, does the other tend to rise (positive), fall (negative), or not move together (near zero)?
Heavier cars generally get fewer mpg. Shoe size and exam score have no mechanism linking them.

feedback.wrong: Negative r means y tends to fall as x rises.

solution.body: **(a) negative** — heavier → fewer mpg. **(b) positive** — more study → higher scores. **(c) near zero** — no meaningful link.
solution.takeaway: The sign of r encodes direction of association; near zero is what you expect when variables are genuinely unrelated.

steps.title: Correlation is not causation
steps.context: A town finds **r = 0.86** between monthly ice-cream sales and drowning deaths. A columnist says ice cream causes drownings.
steps.prompt: What's the main flaw in that conclusion?

options.label: Correlation doesn't prove causation — a third variable may drive both
r = 0.86 is too weak to conclude anything
Drownings cause people to buy ice cream

hints: High r means two things move together — it says nothing about one causing the other.
What changes across summer months that would push both ice-cream sales and swimming (hence drownings) up together?

feedback.wrong: Association ≠ cause. Look for a confounding variable.

solution.body: The flaw is inferring **causation from correlation**. **Hot summer weather** independently raises ice-cream sales and swimming activity (hence drownings). Neither causes the other.
solution.takeaway: Correlation establishes association, not cause — always ask whether a confounding variable could produce the link.

steps.title: Live correlation sandbox
steps.prompt: Drag points on the scatterplot and watch **r**, **r²**, and the regression line update live. Click the plot to place your own outlier — see how much one point can move the line.

feedback.ready: Sign from direction, strength from tightness — and one outlier can hijack the whole line.

steps.title: Repair cost vs car age
steps.context: Annual repair cost ($) plotted against car age (years), with the least-squares line **ŷ = 200 + 45x**.
steps.prompt: Study the regression line, then interpret its slope and intercept on the next page.

feedback.ready: The line climbs about $45 for each additional year — that steady rise is the slope.

steps.title: Interpret slope and intercept
steps.context:
Regression of annual repair cost ($) on car age (years):
**ŷ = 200 + 45x**
steps.prompt: What does the **slope (45)** mean in plain language?

options.label: Each extra year adds $45 to predicted annual repair cost
A brand-new car costs $45/year to repair
Total cost after 45 years is $200

hints: In ŷ = a + bx, the slope b is the change in predicted y for each one-unit increase in x. Keep the units attached.
The intercept 200 is predicted cost at age 0 — the slope is the per-year change.

feedback.wrong: Slope = per-unit change in y; intercept = predicted y when x = 0.

solution.body: **Slope = $45/year** — each additional year adds $45 to predicted annual repair cost. **Intercept = $200** — predicted cost for a 0-year-old car (only meaningful if x = 0 is near the data).
solution.takeaway: Slope = per-unit change in y; intercept = predicted y at x = 0 — always state units and treat the intercept cautiously.

steps.title: Prediction within range
steps.prompt: Predict the annual repair cost for a **6-year-old** car using **ŷ = 200 + 45x** (so x = 6).
hints: 200 + 45(6). Since 6 is inside 1–12, this is safe interpolation.

solution.body: **ŷ = 200 + 45(6) = $470/year.** Because 6 years is within the data range, this is **interpolation** and is trustworthy.
solution.takeaway: Substituting an x inside the observed range is interpolation and generally safe.

steps.title: The danger of extrapolation
steps.context: Same model **ŷ = 200 + 45x** (data ages 1–12). A manager predicts cost for a **25-year-old** car: **$1,325**.

options.label: 25 years is far outside the data — the linear pattern isn't validated there
They plugged the formula in wrong
Linear models work forever — $2,000 is fine

hints: 25 years is far outside the 1–12 range the line was fit on.
Real costs may curve, jump, or level off — the model was never tested that far out.

feedback.wrong: Extrapolation means predicting where you have no evidence.

solution.body: **Extrapolation** — 25 years is far beyond the observed 1–12 range. The linear pattern may break entirely (cars scrapped, major overhauls, etc.).
solution.takeaway: Never trust predictions far outside the range of the data — the model carries no evidence about behavior it never saw.

steps.title: Interpret r²
steps.context: Regression of exam score on hours studied: **r = 0.7**.
steps.prompt: What is r² (the coefficient of determination)?
hints: r² is literally r squared — the coefficient of determination.
0.7² = ? Convert to the proportion of variation in y explained by x.

solution.body: **r² = 0.49 (49%).** About 49% of score variation is explained by hours studied; the other 51% is sleep, prior knowledge, test difficulty, and chance.
solution.takeaway: r² measures explanatory power — a respectable r = 0.7 still explains under half the variation.

steps.title: Tutoring center scatterplot
steps.context:
Six students at a tutoring center:
**Hours:** 10, 12, 14, 16, 18, 20
**Scores:** 70, 72, 78, 80, 85, 88
steps.prompt: Study the scatterplot and regression line before answering the capstone questions.

feedback.ready: Points rise in a tight band — strong positive association with r near +0.99.

steps.title: Association strength
steps.prompt: Is the association **positive or negative**, and roughly how strong?

options.label: Strong positive — y rises steadily with x
Weak positive
Negative

hints: Picture the points — do they rise or fall, and how tightly?
Scores climb with practice hours in an almost straight band.

feedback.wrong: Points rise steadily and nearly linearly.

solution.body: **Strong positive** association — r ≈ +0.99. y rises steadily with x in a tight band.
solution.takeaway: Name direction and strength before computing — the scatterplot tells you both.

steps.title: Predict within range
steps.prompt: Predict the exam score for **15 hours** of tutoring (points).

options.label: 79 points
52 points
27 points
70 points

hints: Substitute x = 15. Is 15 inside the observed range?

solution.body: **ŷ ≈ 79 points** (51.5 + 1.83×15 ≈ 78.95). x = 15 is inside 10–20 → reliable interpolation.
solution.takeaway: Predict only inside the data range unless you're explicitly discussing extrapolation risk.

steps.title: Why not 60 hours?
steps.prompt: Give one reason **not** to use this line to predict score after **60 hours**.

options.label: 60 hours is far outside the data — extrapolation
The slope formula is wrong
No reason — linear trends always hold

hints: Compare 60 to the largest observed x (20).
Scores can't exceed 100; the linear trend must flatten well before 60.

feedback.wrong: 60 is triple the observed range — that's extrapolation.

solution.body: **60 hours is extrapolation** (observed range 10–20). Scores can't exceed 100 and the linear trend must flatten — the prediction is meaningless.
solution.takeaway: A good analysis names direction/strength, predicts inside the data range, and flags extrapolation as unreliable.

steps.title: Chapter 2 complete
steps.body:
You matched scatterplots to r, interpreted slopes, predicted safely inside the data, and squared r for explanatory power.

*Next up:* probability — complements, overlap, independence, and trees.
